issues.title: bibs: update_marc_field silently deletes repeated instances of a repeatable MARC field (data loss)
issues.body_raw:
**Severity:** 1 — silent data loss on live records
**Priority:** High
**Effort:** M
**Type:** Bug
**Found by:** MARC 21 compliance review 2026-07-12 (`/expert-cataloger`)
**Affected code:** `src/almaapitk/domains/bibs.py`
- `BibliographicRecords.update_marc_field` (~L461)
- `BibliographicRecords._build_updated_marc_xml` (~L516)

## Problem
`_build_updated_marc_xml` rebuilds the record and replaces the target tag **once** (at its first occurrence), then `continue`s past every other occurrence of the same tag — so all further occurrences are dropped from the XML that is PUT back to Alma.

## MARC rule
6XX, 5XX, 7XX, 020, 490, 856, etc. are **Repeatable (R)** (LOC MARC 21 Bibliographic Format — general rule: every field may repeat unless its R/NR label precludes it). A record routinely carries several 650s.

## Failure scenario
Record has three subject headings:
- `650 _0 $a Physics`
- `650 _0 $a Astrophysics`
- `650 _0 $a Cosmology`

Call: `update_marc_field(mms_id, "650", {"a": "Physics -- data reduction"})`
Result: **two subject headings are deleted**; the record is left with a single 650. No error, no warning.

## Fix (options)
- Preserve untargeted occurrences of the tag.
- Add an explicit `mode` = `replace_first` | `replace_all` | `append`, defaulting to a non-destructive behavior.
- Provide a way to target a specific occurrence.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Updating one instance of a repeated tag preserves the other instances.
- [ ] There is a supported way to append an additional instance of a repeatable field without deleting existing ones.
- [ ] R10 regression test under `tests/unit/regressions/` reproduces the 3→1 collapse; fails on current `main`, passes after the fix.

## Release gating
Blocks any new PyPI release (`release-blocker`).
acceptance_criteria: Updating one instance of a repeated tag preserves the other instances.
There is a supported way to append an additional instance of a repeatable field without deleting existing ones.
R10 regression test under `tests/unit/regressions/` reproduces the 3→1 collapse; fails on current `main`, passes after the fix.

issues.title: bibs: MARC editing path cannot represent repeated subfield codes (dict-typed subfields)
issues.body_raw:
**Severity:** 1 — cannot represent valid MARC; silent data loss
**Priority:** High
**Effort:** M
**Type:** Bug
**Found by:** MARC 21 compliance review 2026-07-12 (`/expert-cataloger`)
**Affected code:** `src/almaapitk/domains/bibs.py`
- `BibliographicRecords._add_datafield` (~L569, `subfields: Dict[str, str]`)
- reached via `update_marc_field` (~L461)

## Problem
The editing path types subfields as a `Dict[str, str]` and iterates `.items()`. A Python dict cannot hold two entries with the same key, so a repeated subfield code is impossible — the second value silently overwrites the first at dict-construction time, before the code even runs.

## MARC rule
Subfield codes repeat routinely: `650 _0 $a Science $x History $x 20th century`, `700 ... $e author $e editor`, `020 $z ... $z ...` (LOC MARC 21 Bibliographic Format; "every subfield may repeat unless R/NR precludes it").

## Contrast (the correct pattern already exists)
The **creation** builder `build_alma_bib_xml` types subfields as a **list of `[code, value]` pairs** (~L153), which preserves both order and repetition. The editing path should adopt the same shape.

## Failure scenario
Building `650 _0 $a Science $x History $x 20th century` via `update_marc_field` is impossible; only the last `$x` survives.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The editing path accepts an ordered list of `[code, value]` pairs (not a dict).
- [ ] Repeated subfield codes round-trip correctly.
- [ ] R10 regression test under `tests/unit/regressions/` covers a field with two `$x` subfields.

## Release gating
Blocks any new PyPI release (`release-blocker`).
acceptance_criteria: The editing path accepts an ordered list of `[code, value]` pairs (not a dict).
R10 regression test under `tests/unit/regressions/` covers a field with two `$x` subfields.

issues.title: bibs: double-escaping of ampersand/lt/gt corrupts field content in MARC editing path
issues.body_raw:
**Severity:** 1 — corrupts field content
**Priority:** High
**Effort:** S
**Type:** Bug
**Found by:** MARC 21 compliance review 2026-07-12 (`/expert-cataloger`)
**Affected code:** `src/almaapitk/domains/bibs.py`
- `BibliographicRecords._sanitize_xml_text` (~L582)
- reached via `update_marc_field` → `_build_updated_marc_xml` → `_add_datafield`

## Problem
`_sanitize_xml_text` manually rewrites `&`→`&`, `<`→`<`, `>`→`>`, etc., **and then** the value is assigned to `.text` and re-serialized by `ElementTree`, which escapes it **again**. Each special character is therefore escaped twice.

## Failure scenario
Update a 245 to `Law & order`. The value stored in Alma becomes `Law & order` — the ampersand is mangled in the actual bibliographic data. (Same for `<`, `>`.)

## Root cause / context
This is exactly the double-escape bug that issue #179 fixed for the **creation** path: `_strip_illegal_xml_chars` (~L13) deliberately does **not** pre-escape, letting `ElementTree` escape exactly once. That fix was never applied to the **editing** path, so the two helpers now coexist and only `_strip_illegal_xml_chars` is correct.

## Fix
Remove the manual `&/</>/"/'` replacement from the editing path; route it through `_strip_illegal_xml_chars` (strip illegal control chars only, let ET escape once). Consider deleting `_sanitize_xml_text` entirely.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A field value containing `&`, `<`, `>` round-trips through `update_marc_field` unmangled.
- [ ] R10 regression test under `tests/unit/regressions/` asserts a single level of escaping (e.g. `Law & order` stays `Law & order`).

## Release gating
Blocks any new PyPI release (`release-blocker`).
acceptance_criteria: A field value containing `&`, `<`, `>` round-trips through `update_marc_field` unmangled.
R10 regression test under `tests/unit/regressions/` asserts a single level of escaping (e.g. `Law & order` stays `Law & order`).
